reasoning: The threshold to beat is the 30-day average of fatalities over the preceding 360 days, which is currently ~0.583 (corresponding to 7 fatalities in the past year). Because fatalities are integers, the question essentially asks whether there will be at least 1 fatality in the 30-day window leading up to each resolution date. Assuming the 7 fatalities were spread across ~4-6 independent incidents (mostly landmines or minor border shootings), the baseline mathematical probability of seeing >=1 fatality in an average 30-day window is between 28% and 38%. For the first window (March 7 to April 5), 23 days have already passed without any media reports of fatal landmine or political violence incidents. With only 7 days remaining, the probability is low (~12%). For future full 30-day windows, the probability starts at current base rates (~34%) and drifts slowly upward towards 0.5 to reflect the increasing uncertainty over larger horizons and the persistent tail risk of border flare-ups.
